data.title: Hong Kong cook jailed 18 years for ‘evil’ sexual abuse of daughter
data.description:
A Hong Kong cook has been jailed for 18 years for persistently sexually abusing the daughter he shares with his mistress, also slammed by the judge for trying to keep the victim silent about the defendant’s crimes.
The High Court on Wednesday said a deterrent sentence was necessary for the 61-year-old to reflect society’s abhorrence of the “evil” crimes he committed against the girl when she was aged 11 to 18.
The accused, identified only by his initials TML to protect his daughter’s identity, pleaded guilty last year to six counts of rape and three of indecent assault.
The married man is a father of six, three of whom were born to his mistress from mainland China.
He has three previous convictions, including one for indecent assault in 1987, but had never been jailed before this case.
The court heard the defendant raped his daughter, X, and molested her twice at her home in Haifeng county, Guangdong, in May and June 2015, when she was 11.
He gave the girl three HK$100 (US$12.80) banknotes as “pocket money” after the assaults, but she refused.
He went on to rape X five more times and assault her once between September 2021 and March 2022, after she began staying at her elder brother’s flat in Hung Hom to pursue interior design studies.
Three of those incidents took place when X was unable to leave the flat due to a 14-day home quarantine requirement during the Covid-19 pandemic.
The girl disclosed her father’s abuse to her mother in April 2022. The mother, initially angry, told X not to report the matter to police to protect her reputation.
In a final cry for justice, the teenager left a note detailing the abuse before attempting suicide in October 2023. Police arrested the defendant on the same day she was rescued.
A victim impact report revealed the lifelong psychological harm suffered by the complainant, now 22.
Clinical psychologist Chan Sin-ting, of the Social Welfare Department, said the defendant’s acts were systematic, calculated and intrusive, as he exploited every opportunity to violate his daughter during the vulnerable developmental stage of her life.
“He had single-handedly turned her home into a place of fear and threat,” Chan was cited as saying.
The court heard that X quit school and retreated into the digital world, avoiding contact with others.
After arriving in Hong Kong, she remained profoundly isolated, struggling to form friendships and maintain social ties, and often experienced mood swings and difficulty concentrating at work.
While the complainant had hoped her elder brother would provide protection, he misinterpreted the defendant’s conduct as paternal affection, reinforcing her fear that disclosure would lead to disbelief and dismissal.
The report also said the complainant’s mother failed to give sufficient concern for her daughter’s well-being, causing her despair when she invalidated her revelation of the defendant’s acts and prioritised familial stability over justice.
Even at the time of the assessment, X remained ambivalent about reporting her father to authorities, knowing his financial support was critical to her family.
Chan said X required intensive, trauma-specific psychotherapy to recover.
Madam Justice Esther Toh Lye-ping said the facts of the case were “deeply painful and heartbreaking”.
“Every parent in our community will find this case abhorrent. I must say the mother of X should also be thoroughly ashamed of herself for putting financial concern above her well-being,” Toh said.
“I recognise also that no matter how long the imprisonment of the defendant, it would never be sufficient to compensate Ms X for the pain and abuse she had suffered.
“However, the sentence has to reflect society’s abhorrence for these offences and send a message that the court stands readily to protect the vulnerable children in our community.”
The court jailed the defendant for 12 years and 1½ years for each count of rape and indecent assault, respectively, before ordering a partially consecutive sentence, for a total of 18 years.
Rape carries a maximum sentence of life imprisonment, while indecent assault is punishable by up to 10 years behind bars.

data.title: HKEX posts record quarterly profit, topping forecast as listings, turnover surge
data.description:
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX), operator of Asia’s third-largest stock market, reported a record quarterly profit in the first three months of 2026, buoyed by more new listings and increased turnover.
Net profit rose 27 per cent to HK$5.19 billion (US$662 million) – or HK$4.10 per share – from a year earlier, the company said on Wednesday. The result beat the HK$4.6 billion consensus among analysts.
It is the highest quarterly profit on record, surpassing the HK$4.9 billion in the third quarter of 2025.
“HKEX delivered a strong start to 2026 with revenue and profit both reaching record highs in the first quarter, as global capital continued to seek safe havens and access to Asian growth opportunities in a volatile macro environment,” CEO Bonnie Chan Yiting said in a statement.
“Beyond equities, our diversified multi-asset platform continued to perform strongly. The LME [London Metal Exchange] saw record chargeable trading volumes for the quarter, reflecting active participation across global metals markets.”

HKEX shares rose 3 per cent to HK$419.80 at 1.30pm on Wednesday, after the results announcement. They have risen 1 per cent this year, better than the benchmark Hang Seng Index, which dropped 1 per cent during the same period.
“It was a strong set of HKEX results that should drive consensus earnings upgrades, though the investment income beat is unlikely to be fully extrapolated,” said Michael Zhang, an analyst at Citi Research, in a report after the announcement.
Revenue and other income in the first ­quarter jumped 20 per cent to a record HK$8.2 billion, beating the market estimate of HK$7.6 billion.
Daily stock trading turnover surged 14 per cent from a year earlier to HK$276.7 billion, lifting trading fees by 15 per cent and clearing fees by 18 per cent.
The ­average daily turnover of the Stock Connect’s southbound channel rose 11 per cent to HK$122.5 billion, while the northbound channel recorded a 70 per cent jump to 324.1 billion yuan (US$47.4 billion), HKEX data showed.
Listing fees increased by 37 per cent. HKEX’s main board ranked first globally for funds raised by new listings, with 37 companies raising US$13.26 billion in the first quarter, according to LSEG Data and Analytics. That represented a 453 per cent increase from the same period in 2025. One GEM initial public offering raised US$9 million, bringing the total for all boards to the highest first-quarter tally in five years.
HKEX also booked a HK$441 million net investment gain from its equity and bond portfolio, down 15 per cent from a year earlier.
Trading in derivatives warrants and other products rose 24 per cent to HK$21.4 billion per day. The LME, wholly owned by HKEX, handled 877,000 lots of metal contracts per day during the quarter, a 26 per cent increase from a year earlier.

data.title: Government vows action as landlord-tenant rows block access at Wang Fuk Court
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have pledged to intervene in landlord-tenant disputes preventing residents from retrieving belongings from the fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court, as packing efforts enter their 10th day.
Deputy Chief Secretary Warner Cheuk Wing-hing said on Wednesday that while the government expected agreement on limited entry quotas, authorities were prepared to make “alternative arrangements” to ensure all residents had a fair chance to retrieve their belongings.
“Our stance on this is very clear. The purpose of arranging access for residents is to allow them to retrieve their belongings,” Cheuk said. “If a consensus cannot be reached, the government will step in to ensure tenants are given a reasonable opportunity to return and collect their belongings.”
The Social Welfare Department said that while some disputes had occurred, the vast majority of cases involving conflicts between landlords and tenants had already been mediated by frontline social workers.
“If there are cases where, for example, residents still have requests or require further assistance after visiting their units, the department will continue to support and assist them in a manner that balances compassion, reason and the law,” said Director of Social Welfare Edward To Wing-hang.

In the nine days up to Tuesday, residents of four blocks – Wang Shing, Wang Yan, Wang Tao and Wang Cheung Houses – visited their flats to pack belongings under government arrangements.
A total of 951 households comprising 3,464 people accessed their flats, with 401 households, involving 894 people, returning more than once.
The inferno that broke out on November 26 killed 168 people, displaced about 5,000 residents and destroyed all but one of Wang Fuk Court’s eight blocks in Tai Po.
On Wednesday, residents of Wang Tai House and Wang Kin House were the latest batch to return and pack their belongings.
Eighty-two of the deaths occurred at Wang Tai House, with most bodies found in the stairwell. Firefighter Ho Wai-ho was found on open ground outside the block’s lobby, while some of his gear was recovered on the 31st floor.
Another 14 residents were injured, and 58 per cent of flats were significantly damaged by the fire.
Wang Tai House will remain open to residents until May 4, with only five floors accessible each day, while Wang Kin House, which was less severely damaged, will remain open until Friday.
Additional reporting by Lo Hoi-ying

data.title: China Resources Power’s renewable energy arm cleared for Shenzhen IPO
data.description:
China Resources Power (CRP), one of the largest mainland power producers listed in Hong Kong, has received approval to spin off its renewable energy arm for a separate listing in Shenzhen, marking a key step in a years-long plan to tap capital markets amid strong demand for new energy.
The Shenzhen Stock Exchange’s listing committee approved the proposed A-share offering of China Resources New Energy Holdings on April 28, according to a filing by CRP. The deal, however, still required registration approval from the China Securities Regulatory Commission and remained subject to market conditions, the company said.
The approval marks a step forward for a plan first announced in March 2023, bringing the state-backed power producer close to the final stage after roughly three years of preparation.
China Resources New Energy, which houses the group’s wind and solar assets, is seeking to raise about 24.5 billion yuan (US$3.6 billion) from the initial public offering (IPO), potentially making it one of the largest new energy listings in Shenzhen in recent years. The proceeds would be used to fund the construction of wind and solar projects, according to the company’s prospectus.

The unit has built a sizeable nationwide footprint. As of the end of 2025, its installed capacity reached about 41.6 gigawatts, with wind accounting for the majority and solar making up the rest, the prospectus showed. Its power plants spanned 31 provinces, positioning it among the leading renewable energy operators in China.
Revenue rose to 22.9 billion yuan in 2025 from 20.5 billion yuan in 2023, while net profit declined to 6.1 billion yuan from 8.28 billion yuan over the same period, according to its prospectus.
The listing comes amid sustained investor appetite for renewable energy assets, driven by China’s long-term decarbonisation targets and policy support. It also underscores a broader trend among central state-owned enterprises to separately list their renewable businesses to unlock value and secure dedicated funding channels.
China Three Gorges Renewables completed a blockbuster listing in 2021, followed by China Huadian’s renewable unit, Huadian New Energy Group, in 2025, while PowerChina’s green energy arm was also preparing a similar listing in Shanghai, targeting a fundraising of about 9 billion yuan.
The listing also marked a breakthrough in China’s capital market reforms. China Resources New Energy is set to become the first red-chip company – firms incorporated offshore but with most operations in China – to pass a listing hearing on Shenzhen’s main board. Previous red-chip listings in the A-share market have largely taken place in Shanghai, either on the main board or the Star Market.

data.title: Amber rainstorm warning issued as thunderstorms hit Hong Kong
data.description:
This story has been made freely available as a public service to our readers. Please consider supporting SCMP’s journalism by subscribing.
Hong Kong’s weather forecaster issued an amber rainstorm warning on Wednesday calling on the public to seek safe shelter as thunderstorms and intense gusts might hit the city.
The Hong Kong Observatory raised the signal at 11.35am and said areas of intense thundery showers associated with upper-air disturbances had been persistently affecting the area.
The alert was cancelled at 12.45pm.
The forecaster warned that flooding could occur in some low-lying and poorly drained areas, with heavy rain possibly bringing flash floods.
It issued a thunderstorm warning at 9.20am, adding that intense gusts reaching 70km/h (43.5mph) or above might affect Hong Kong.
It also advised the public to seek shelter as soon as possible and stay away from highly conductive objects, trees and masts.
Temperatures are expected to fall to as low as 19 degrees Celsius (66.2 Fahrenheit) on Thursday as a cold front over inland Guangdong edges southwards.

data.title: Challenges ahead for Wang Fuk Court homeowners who reject buy-back offer
data.description:
Homeowners of the only block undamaged in the deadly Wang Fuk Court inferno who decline the government’s buy-back offer will face significant challenges in the management and maintenance of their properties in the long run, Hong Kong authorities have warned.
Deputy Financial Secretary Michael Wong Wai-lun said on Wednesday that while the government respected homeowners’ decisions, the possibility for authorities to invoke legislation to acquire all property titles in Wang Chi House “absolutely exists”.
The government said on Tuesday that it might offer to buy back the 248 flats at Wang Chi House originally excluded from a resettlement plan for the other seven fire-ravaged blocks in the estate, if at least three-quarters of homeowners agreed to it. About 77 per cent of them earlier indicated their initial interest in joining the buy-back scheme.
Wong on Wednesday said the acquisition proposal would officially be extended to the block if at least 75 per cent of owners signed an official letter of acceptance by June 30.
He added that homeowners who chose not to sell their properties to the government could face major difficulties in maintaining or selling the flats in the future.
“The land lease and the Deed of Mutual Covenant are very difficult to handle. They need the approval from the owners of all 1,984 flats [of Wang Fuk Court] before they can amend the terms,” he noted.
“They will also have repairs to deal with and will need to contribute funds, and I understand that these shared costs will be quite substantial. Management fees in the future may be different as they would shift from being shared among eight blocks to being borne by Wang Chi House solely as a stand-alone building.”
While the acquisition was currently proceeding on a voluntary basis, Wong said legislation could not be ruled out for the resumption of titles at Wang Chi House.
“This possibility absolutely exists. We must plan for the future. If the government decides to use legislative means to recover the remaining units of Wang Chi House, having at least 75 per cent of the units in our possession would pave the way for those future efforts.”
If the government indeed needed to push ahead with the legislation, which he acknowledged could face judicial challenges, Wong said it would only take place next year at the earliest due to its complexities.
The resettlement plan first unveiled in February sets buy-back prices for flats without land premiums at HK$8,000 (US$1,020) per square foot, while those with premiums would be valued at HK$10,500 per square foot.
Residents can choose a cash buyout or a “flat-for-flat” arrangement to exchange their homes for new subsidised flats at 10 designated projects under the Housing Authority or the Housing Society through a special sales scheme.
Secretary for Housing Winnie Ho Wing-yin said on Wednesday that a significant majority of the 3,900 units offered under the scheme – about 2,400 flats – exceeded 400 sq ft, similar to the size of the flats in Wang Fuk Court.
Lawmaker Bill Tang Ka-piu of the Federation of Trade Unions said some residents were concerned that the 75 per cent threshold for signing the letter of acceptance might be too high.

“While the government has reached out to 99 per cent of owners and noted that 77 per cent expressed a willingness to accept the proposal, some owners worried that if a few per cent of them fail to sign or submit documents, the plan could reach a stalemate,” Tang said.
“If the threshold can be lowered to 70 per cent, it will make owners more at ease.”
Tang also acknowledged that while the June 30 deadline was set to be consistent with the other seven affected blocks, authorities should allow for some flexibility.
“While residents in other blocks had ample time to research the 10 available estates for ‘flat-for-flat’ exchanges, Wang Chi House residents only recently confirmed their eligibility and now have only two months to make life-changing decisions,” he said.
Asked whether any discretionary treatment would apply if the threshold was narrowly missed, Wong indicated that appropriate consideration would be given on a case-by-case basis. For instance, flexibility would be granted if a resident was unable to sign in time due to flight delays.
The inclusion of Wang Chi House under the resettlement scheme would cost the government HK$1 billion, on top of the roughly HK$6.8 billion needed to purchase the homes in the seven towers destroyed in the blaze.
The blaze that broke out on November 26 last year killed 168 people, including a firefighter, and displaced about 5,000 residents.

data.title: If it can’t stop the Meta-Manus AI deal, how can Beijing exert its authority?
data.description:
It could be “time-consuming”, “complex” and “difficult” for Meta Platforms to unwind its acquisition of Manus, an artificial intelligence start-up that originated in China, given how far the deal has gone, according to analysts.
Beijing’s order on Monday blocking the US$2 billion deal came roughly four months after the acquisition was announced. During that time, Manus – developer of what it described as the world’s first general AI agent – provided Meta employees with unlimited-usage accounts, and some Meta teams held meetings and exchanged ideas with Manus staff members, according to a Meta employee.
The integration proceeded even though Beijing announced a review of the transaction days after its announcement. The Manus team members moved into Meta’s offices in Singapore and were granted Meta corporate accounts and other access, the South China Morning Post reported in February.
As Manus members have been integrated into Meta, “disentangling them ... is going to be tricky”, said Paul Triolo, senior vice-president for China and technology policy lead at DGA-Albright Stonebridge Group.
“Unwinding the deal in practice will be time-consuming and complex,” said Yuwen Pei, a partner at law firm Lifeng Partners, adding that once Manus’ core technology had been absorbed into Meta’s ecosystem, restoring the status quo would be extremely difficult.
“Given the acquisition has gone through, with employees and assets already integrated, and investors paid, it’s difficult to see how an unwinding would be accomplished,” said Tom Nunlist, associate director of tech and data policy at research consultancy Trivium China.
Manus investors including Tencent Holdings, ZhenFund and HongShan Capital, previously known as Sequoia China, had received their proceeds, according to a Bloomberg report on Monday. None of the firms immediately responded to requests for comment on Tuesday.
Unwinding the deal could involve equity reversals, asset disposals, termination of intellectual property licences, system disconnections, data access revocations and personnel reshuffling, said Hunter Su, a compliance expert at the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Technology Association.
Yuwen said challenges included the movement of key staff and know-how, as well as disputes over what a “complete separation” of technology and data would mean, likening the complexity to ByteDance’s years-long efforts to separate TikTok.
If the parties involved in the deal “completely ignore their development in the China market and force the merger, the government cannot completely stop the transaction”, said Wei Hongxu, a researcher at Beijing-based think tank Anbound.
However, Beijing could exert its authority by imposing restrictions on Manus’ financial accounts, data outflow and employees’ travel, Wei added.


Beijing set a preliminary deadline of several weeks to reverse the transaction before it would consider punishing the relevant parties, according to a Monday report by the Wall Street Journal.
Wei warned that sanctions could deal a blow to Manus’ valuation, as the AI industry was developing rapidly.
Manus could be revalued as agent frameworks like OpenClaw had gained traction, with industry focus shifting towards open, local and replaceable models for personal agent systems, Su said.
Manus rose to fame in March 2025 after releasing its agent of the same name. But the AI industry has transformed dramatically since then.
“The emergence of other major approaches to agentic platforms, such as OpenClaw and others, means that while Manus last year appeared to be at least at the forefront or one of the leaders in putting together agentic AI platforms, calling tools and other things, now [it is] in a very different world,” Triolo said, adding that the value of Manus personnel to Meta “may be less salient than it was a year ago”.
Manus initially operated in Beijing and Wuhan, but moved to Singapore by mid-June 2025, laying off some China-based staff and shutting down its Chinese social-media accounts.
Yuwen said the case was likely to serve as a cautionary tale for other Chinese companies that pursued foreign investment or cross-border deals. They should assess filing obligations earlier, include regulatory-veto exit clauses and track export control and data transfer rules, she added.
According to Lu Shengyun, founder of Shanghai-based LSY Consulting, the case showed that US-China AI competition was no longer just about chips or models but had broadened into areas of engineering and product design.
“Particularly in the age of agentic AI, we’re seeing growing competition in areas like data and engineering harnesses,” he said, referring to the fact that Manus does not train its own models, but rather develops “harnesses”, which are the frameworks that turn AI models into functional agents.

data.title: Chinese industrial robot maker Inovance joins Hong Kong IPO queue
data.description:
Chinese industrial robot maker Shenzhen Inovance Technology submitted an initial public offering (IPO) application in Hong Kong on Tuesday, joining a wave of mainland tech firms seeking capital from international investors.
Founded in 2003, Inovance is seen as a cornerstone of China’s industrial automation sector. The company’s shares, listed on the Shenzhen Stock Exchange since 2010, closed at 61.36 yuan on Tuesday, giving it a market capitalisation of nearly 175.3 billion yuan (US$25.6 billion).
Positioning itself as a high-end equipment service provider, Inovance focuses on five layers of core technology: information, control, drive, execution and sensing, according to its website.
In January, Inovance announced its plan to issue shares in Hong Kong to advance its internationalisation strategy, enhance its global brand image and diversify its financing channels. It was reported in late March that the company had picked sponsors for the deal, expecting to raise as much as US$2 billion.
In its filing with the Hong Kong stock exchange, Inovance said that its revenue reached 45 billion yuan last year, an increase of 21.8 per cent from a year earlier, while its net profit grew nearly 18 per cent to 5.05 billion yuan.
In September, the company successfully spun off a subsidiary focused on electric-vehicle components. Inovance Automotive raised 3.6 billion yuan on the Shenzhen Stock Exchange to fund overseas expansion and development of electric drive systems.
Inovance Technology’s Hong Kong IPO application comes as the city’s primary market has regained momentum and reignited enthusiasm among international investors in China’s tech sector.
In late March, retail shares available in the listing of Guangdong Huayan Robotics were oversubscribed more than 5,000 times, though the company only logged an 8 per cent gain on its debut in Hong Kong.
Shanghai Xizhi Technology made a spectacular debut on Tuesday, with its stock price surging more than 380 per cent to HK$886. The company develops optical interconnect and optical computing products designed to accelerate artificial intelligence infrastructure.

data.title: ‘No fundraising launched’ after father’s death, dancer Mo Li warns of scams
data.description:
A Hong Kong dancer left paralysed after being struck by a giant screen during a 2022 concert by popular boy band Mirror has clarified that neither he nor his family had raised any funds after the death of his pastor father.
Reminding his supporters to beware of scammers, Mo Li Kai-yin said in an Instagram post on Tuesday that he was grateful for the concern shown in recent days over online reports about his family, following the death of his father, Reverend Derek Li Shing-lam.
But he added: “We as a family have not used the recent matter to raise funds through any organisations.”
“I urge all kind‑hearted people: if you come across articles that include fundraising information from certain organisations, please check carefully who the recipient is. Thank you for your care.”
The death of the dancer’s father, who spent years advocating for his 31-year-old son after the accident that shocked the city, was announced on Saturday.

About a week earlier, the elder Li revealed on his Facebook page that he was undergoing treatment for an undisclosed illness.
He had also used the account to provide regular updates on his son in recent years through biweekly prayer letters.
Earlier posts had indicated that Li had been suffering from physical discomfort and had repeatedly raised doctors’ concerns about a possible cardiovascular issue.
Mo Li suffered serious spinal cord injuries after a four-by-four-metre (13-by-13-foot), 560kg (1,235lb) overhead video screen crashed onto the stage at Hong Kong Coliseum on July 28, 2022, after a supporting cable broke.
In March this year, the son shared a video showing himself moving independently in a wheelchair for the first time since the accident.

data.title: Will Hong Kong vapers go underground as public possession ban kicks in?
data.description:
Twenty-two-year-old Hongkonger Elio* started smoking at the age of 16, when he was studying abroad in New Zealand.
But what started as a way to kill time soon became a daily habit to relieve the stress of an uncertain future.
Despite suffering from a collapsed lung in 2020, he resumed smoking and drinking a few months after recovery, later switching to e-cigarettes amid rising tobacco taxes.
After the 2022 ban on the sale of vapes, he restricted his habit to one capsule every three days – equivalent to 20 cigarettes in terms of nicotine content – but continued to purchase pods online to sustain his supply.
He vapes only in private to avoid enforcement officers.
“If I haven’t vaped in a while, I feel really down and lethargic,” he said.
With Hong Kong set to ban the possession of alternative tobacco products in public places from Thursday, Elio remains undeterred and plans to continue vaping in private.
“I have no plans to quit cigarettes at all. I’m not hurting anyone, so why is vaping treated like doing drugs?” he asked.
From April 30, anyone found to be carrying more than five vape pods or 100 heat sticks in public will face a maximum fine of HK$50,000 and up to six months in jail, while those with smaller quantities will receive a fixed penalty of HK$3,000.
Some users of alternative tobacco products told the South China Morning Post that they would continue sourcing supplies through illicit channels and vape behind closed doors, with no intention of quitting.
Hong Kong’s overall smoking rate for those aged 15 and above was 8.5 per cent in 2025, while daily vape use stood below 1 per cent in 2023.
In the 2022-23 academic year, 1.1 per cent of secondary students and 0.5 per cent of Primary Four to Six pupils used e-cigarettes.
The new rules follow a 2022 citywide ban on the import, manufacture, sale, distribution and promotion of alternative tobacco products.
The amended law also provides for a future territory-wide ban – including private places – on these products, though no timetable has been set.

A Smoker’s Insight survey in April found that among 301 alternative tobacco users, 55 per cent intend to continue vaping discreetly, while 35 per cent plan to switch back to traditional cigarettes.
Only 10 per cent said they would – or would at least consider – kicking the habit.
“Users of alternative tobacco products will simply find other ways to get their supplies, pushing consumption underground. They’ll use them at home or in private spaces rather than on the street,” said Yeung Ho-chuen, a spokesperson for the concern group.
“It is unfair to blame them for corrupting the minors or leading them to use those products, given that only 1 per cent were smoking them.”
To truly encourage quitting tobacco products, authorities need to stop treating them like “villains” and rethink their approach to smoking cessation services, Yeung said.
“Quitting tobacco is just a change of lifestyle. It should not be framed as a huge, painful challenge … if you take a single puff when you are trying to quit, you should not be treated as a failure,” he said.
“Demonising smokers only makes them feel alienated, leading them to believe that they are incapable of quitting – these psychological barriers stem from the lack of empathy and respect towards them.”
He noted that the United Kingdom and New Zealand have integrated vaping into their public health strategies as a less harmful alternative, as it allows smokers to take just a single puff when a craving hits, rather than feeling forced to finish an entire cigarette.
But Henry Tong Sau-chai, chairman of the Hong Kong Council on Smoking and Health, said the new rules sent a “clear signal” about the dangers of alternative tobacco products.
“Some still regard e-cigarettes as a less harmful alternative to traditional cigarettes, which is a misconception, or a misleading claim. The new law is another chance to educate the public about this and encourage quitting,” he said.
With the import ban in place for four years, any legal personal supplies should be exhausted or expired by now, Tong added.
“We hope the government will soon implement the second stage of the possession ban, which covers both public and private areas, especially as vape devices have been used for illegal drugs in recent years,” he said.
He was also confident that the ban would not push smokers back to traditional cigarettes.
“The incentive for those smokers to switch back to traditional cigarettes would be limited with the multipronged approach of the tobacco control strategy, such as banning flavoured tobacco to reduce its appeal as well as the standardised packaging and duty stamp law, which should be passed at the Legislative Council soon.”
He also urged the authorities to step up campaigns to ensure that people were aware that purchasing these products online was also an offence.
* Name changed on request

data.title: Pair of Hong Kong luxury homes sells for US$41 million as market sizzles
data.description:
A wave of demand from mainland Chinese buyers is restoring liquidity to Hong Kong’s trophy home market, enabling owners who have held onto pricey assets for many years to lock in gains.
Among them, mainland property tycoon Zhao Zhijun sold two adjoining units at 8-12 Peak Road for a combined HK$320 million (US$40.8 million), according to Land Registry records, generating a gain of about 86 per cent compared with the purchase price 16 years ago.
Zhao, chairman of mainland investment firm Niverse, and a related party acquired the eighth-floor units at the development, now known as Infinity, for HK$171.8 million in 2010.
The sale comes as deep-pocketed buyers make a clear return to Hong Kong’s high-end housing market after several years of decline. In the first three months of the year, 72 homes worth more than HK$100 million were sold across the city’s primary and secondary markets, up more than 157 per cent year-on-year, according to Centaline data.
The Infinity sale was signed on April 27 through two companies, Chin Mak and Wai Yi, for HK$165 million and HK$155 million, respectively, or about HK$75,000 per square foot. Company searches show both entities are directed by Zhou Mengfan.
The project has undergone extensive upgrades in recent years, helping support valuations in the ultra-prime segment.
Edward Lai, chief senior account manager at Midland Realty, said the property’s layout and unusually high ceilings of about 3.8 metres added to its appeal.
A comparable unit is currently listed at about HK$180 million, or roughly HK$87,300 per square foot, Lai added.
Experienced luxury property investors saw this as an advantageous time to make moves in the market, as they could get good prices for existing assets and then put that money into properties that they identified as having plenty of room to appreciate, Lai said.
Zhao was among a wave of buyers who entered Hong Kong’s luxury market after the 2008 financial crisis, when prices corrected sharply. Land Registry records showed Zhao first attempted to acquire the units in 2008, but the transaction was later terminated.
Hong Kong comic artist and investor Ma Wing-shing recently sold a unit in the same development for about HK$105 million, generating a gain of roughly 77 per cent after buying it in 2009, according to local media reports.
Ma did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Prices for homes of at least 1,076 sq ft rose about 0.9 per cent month on month in March, according to official data. The level remains about 17 per cent below the peak in October 2021.
However, not all investors have benefited. SEA Holdings is expected to book a loss of about HK$17.2 million after agreeing to sell a house at 1 Shouson Hill Road East for HK$180 million, according to a filing, highlighting continued pressure in parts of the luxury market.
Meanwhile, long-established Hong Kong families are also testing sentiment. The Liu Chong Hing family recently put on sale 94 Repulse Bay Road, which comprises six large units previously valued at about HK$1.1 billion.

data.title: Hong Kong star vows to be ‘companion, not mentor’ to protesters arrested in 2019
data.description:
Hong Kong singer Hins Cheung has pledged to be a companion rather than a mentor to youngsters arrested during the 2019 anti-government protests during an event organised as part of a youth rehabilitation programme.
The sharing session, themed “New Opportunity, New Journey”, was held at the Hong Kong Museum of History on Tuesday night.
More than 300 participants attended the event, including youngsters arrested during the protests, as well as university students and members of three neighbourhood committees.
A participant who declined to be named said she was asked to sign a document confirming her attendance.
A staff member from the Hong Kong United Youth Association, the event’s organiser, informed her the document would be passed on to police, adding that some previous participants had later been informed that their cases had been closed.
The 2½-hour event also offered guidance on further studies, career development and life planning, and arranged for participants to visit the National Security Exhibition Gallery at the museum.
However, the woman said she found the event uninspiring and doubted authorities would close her case.
“The event focused on career and life planning. They seemed to assume that we broke the law because we had encountered setbacks in our careers,” she said, noting that she had a stable job, and the session did not resonate with her.
The special rehabilitation programme has been running for two years and aims to support the rehabilitation of about 7,000 people arrested during the protests but not prosecuted.
At the event, Cheung shared his “music dream” journey in a 30-minute speech, saying he wanted to help youth avoid life’s detours.
“During my growing-up years, I wanted someone to walk alongside me rather than lecture me,” Cheung said when asked why he decided to become a mentor to the former protestors.
“That is why, at this stage of my life, I hope to be able to walk together with young people.”
The singer, who called himself a mainland artist “lucky” to build his career in Hong Kong, pledged he would launch music projects to connect the Greater Bay Area with the city.
“He said he was not a guide, but a companion with us, when asked by the host why he chose to be a mentor,” the participant said. “He also expressed hope that he could become a bridge connecting the music cultures of the Greater Bay Area and Hong Kong.”
University student Alvin Lau, 19, who was invited after taking part in volunteer work, said Cheung’s story of overcoming failures to become a successful singer inspired him, adding that it was a privilege to have a close encounter with the Cantopop star.
“He spoke about experiencing many failures and how his path had never been smooth. It was a particularly winding road,” Lau said. “He also talked about how to face pressure and the importance of continuing to move forward on our own.”
Several participants said that Cheung did not directly address national security and political issues and the recent controversy over his role as a mentor.
Other speakers included retired tennis player Zhang Ling and Mark Mak Hin-yu, co-founder of the Hong Kong-based tech start-up Roborn Technology.

The Security Bureau’s appointment of Cheung as a mentor drew a backlash from internet users in mainland China, who flooded Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung’s Weibo account, questioning the singer’s suitability for the role.
A bureau spokesman stressed it welcomed individuals from all backgrounds and sectors to share positive life experiences with the youngsters.
Cheung was previously embroiled in a patriotism controversy following his support for protests against national education in 2012 and the Occupy Central civil disobedience movement in 2014, when he sang its theme song with actor Anthony Wong Chau-sang.
But the singer expressed “deep repentance” earlier for his past words and actions, describing some as “inappropriate”, and said he hoped to personally lead youth exchange visits to the mainland and convey a clear and firm stance to the public.

data.title: Helper arrested in Hong Kong for allegedly putting 2 young boys in dog cage
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a domestic helper on suspicion of child abuse after she allegedly put two young boys inside a dog cage in a Tsing Yi flat.
Police said they received a report at 1.34pm on Tuesday from the 26-year-old mother of the boys, aged two and three, who alleged that her helper had confined them in a cage at a flat in the Grand Horizon estate on two occasions in February.
A police source said the mother twice discovered through her mobile phone’s security camera feed that the helper had placed the boys inside the cage while she sat nearby using her phone.
On February 13, the mother saw through her phone two boys were confined inside the cage. On February 21, she saw the youngest one had been put inside.
According to the source, the mother said she did not immediately report the matter until Tuesday when she informed the employment agency and later called police. She said she remained silent fearing harm to her children.
Officers arrested the Indonesian woman, 32, on suspicion of ill-treatment.
She is being detained for investigation.
The case has been handed to the Kwai Tsing district investigation team.
The boys showed no visible injuries and were earlier treated at hospital before being discharged.
The source said the helper had worked for the family for more than two years.
The single mother also has another son, aged four, and keeps a pet dog.

data.title: Rail giant using AR technology to fast-track work on Northern Link station
data.description:
Hong Kong’s railway operator has deployed augmented reality (AR) technology to speed up construction of a station on a new link serving the Northern Metropolis megaproject to ensure it opens on time next year.
The MTR Corporation said on Tuesday that the power supply had been connected at Kwu Tung station and AR technology had been adopted to hasten progress.
“Applying the AR inspection system will allow us to simulate future scenarios and help workers carry out the assembly work accurately and efficiently so that we can ensure our station can open on schedule in 2027,” said Law Tik-ko, head of project delivery for the Northern Link (phase 1).
Several escalators have already been installed at the station using the technology, the South China Morning Post observed.

Kwu Tung station, located between the Sheung Shui and Lok Ma Chau stops on the East Rail line, forms part of the Northern Link.
The link will serve as the transport backbone of the Northern Metropolis megaproject near the border with mainland China.
The project aims to turn 30,000 hectares (74,132 acres) of land into a new engine for economic growth with a population of about 2.5 million and around 650,000 jobs by 2034.
Phase one of the link will run from Kwu Tung station to Kam Sheung Road station on the Tuen Ma line, with three intermediate stations added when phase two is completed. They are San Tin, Ngau Tam Mei and Au Tau stations.
Law said this marked the first time for the MTR Corp to use AR technology in building a station, with plans to apply it in other one in the future.
Workers could use devices such as iPads to visualise the station in real time, helping them assemble components accurately rather than spending hours cross-referencing blueprints and relying on their imagination during construction, he said.
He did not elaborate on manpower saved or how much the system cost.

Authorities previously described the Northern Link as an “engine” of significant strategic value, crucial for providing the cross-border and regional transport needed to maximise the future hub’s potential.
Echoing calls from Beijing’s top man on Hong Kong affairs to remove barriers and accelerate the megaproject, the government and the MTR Corp signed an agreement last year to fast-track the link’s completion to 2034 instead of between 2036 and 2038.
The initial HK$39.05 billion deal, funded by land premiums, covers three mainline stations, a spur line and tunnels.
The project might also involve the participation of mainland Chinese authorities to help excavate tunnels, as part two of the link will involve a station being built at Huanggang at the border.

data.title: Alibaba doubles down on healthcare AI with new tool for early cancer detection
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding has released an artificial intelligence model that it claims is more sensitive than radiologists in spotting early stage colorectal cancer from computed tomography (CT) scans, as the Chinese tech giant doubles down on its efforts to develop AI tools for cancer detection.
Alibaba’s research arm Damo Academy said on Tuesday that its new Coca AI model accurately identified five previously missed cases of colorectal cancer from the non-contrast CT scans of more than 27,000 people, achieving a sensitivity of 86.6 per cent and a specificity of 99.8 per cent. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Sensitivity measures a system’s ability to correctly identify people with a disease, while specificity shows its ability to identify healthy individuals.
Damo Academy said that Coca, developed with Chinese institutions including the Guangdong General Hospital, demonstrated a significantly higher sensitivity compared with 10 radiologists of varying levels of experience, outperforming them by 20.4 per cent.

Current diagnostic methods for colorectal cancer detection, including colonoscopy and CT colonography, were invasive and created discomfort for patients, whereas Coca had the potential to be a non-invasive, cost-effective and scalable tool, Damo researchers said in a paper published in the Annals of Oncology peer-reviewed medical journal last week.
When used to assist physicians, the Coca deep learning model helped improve their sensitivity and specificity by 14.5 per cent and 3.1 per cent respectively, according to Damo.
Coca marked Alibaba’s latest advancement in the use of AI in healthcare. The Damo Academy last year released Grape, an AI model designed to detect gastric cancer, and Panda, which focused on pancreatic cancer.
In April last year, Damo’s Panda model received a “breakthrough device” designation from the US Food and Drug Administration, granting the platform an expedited review and approval process.
Alibaba’s Damo Academy was established in 2017, with focus areas including AI and the open-source chip architecture RISC-V. Last month, Damo unveiled a new flagship RISC-V chip, XuanTie C950, which the research group said was a central processing unit core developed for high-performance tasks in AI computing.
Damo Academy said on Tuesday that it has validated its technical route of combining plain CT and AI models for multi-cancer screening, and that it had made “significant progress” in using AI to screen for five major digestive system cancers: pancreatic, gastric, colorectal, liver and oesophageal. It was also exploring the AI screening of breast and kidney cancer, Damo said.
The team acknowledged in its paper, however, that although the Damo Coca model experiments demonstrated its clinical utility, they were limited to a moderate number of normal pathologically confirmed colorectal cancer cases. Its effectiveness for large-scale screening in real-world settings remained unclear, according to Damo.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest man after fire inside sports store injures staff member
data.description:
A man has been arrested on suspicion of setting fire to clothing inside a Decathlon store in Hong Kong, injuring a shop worker who tried to put out the flames.
Police said the incident was reported at about 3pm at the Telford Plaza store on Wai Yip Street in Kowloon Bay, where the suspect allegedly set fire to some shirts before fleeing.
A 22-year-old male staff member suffered slight burns to his hands while trying to extinguish the flames and was taken to United Christian Hospital in Kwun Tong.
Firefighters quickly put out the fire, which caused HK$1,032 (US$132) worth of damage.
The suspect, described as about 1.7 metres (5 feet, 6 inches) tall and of heavy build, wearing a khaki top, blue trousers and a cap, was later traced to Ying Shun House in nearby Choi Ying Estate, where officers arrested the 40-year-old man at about 5pm.
A police source said the man has a mild intellectual impairment and holds a disability certificate. The case has been classified as arson, with the motive still under investigation.

data.title: Homeowner at Redhill Peninsula fined nearly HK$1 million for illegal structures
data.description:
A Hong Kong court has fined the owner of a luxury home at Redhill Peninsula nearly HK$1 million (US$173,938), the harshest penalty to date for illegal structures discovered during a government inspection at the seaside estate three years ago.
Magistrate Tobias Cheng Yun-chung handed down a HK$980,000 fine on Tuesday on Future Ocean, which has owned No 74 at Redhill Peninsula in Tai Tam since 2006.
Deborah Yuen Wai-ming is listed as the company’s director, and she lives in the house with her husband, local businessman Chan Tin-chi.
Cheng said the case was “extremely serious”, noting the defendant showed “a total disregard for law and safety” by failing to remove the illegal works despite authorities issuing the order to complete the removal by February 2024.
During the hearings, Yuen appeared at Eastern Court on behalf of the company and pleaded not guilty to seven summary offences brought by the Lands Department and Buildings Department.
At Tuesday’s sentencing, Cheng criticised the defendant for “disregarding the law”.
“Apart from escalating the garden from its original ground, all the illegal structures were built to expand the indoor space. The [amount of] unauthorised work is very rampant,” he said.


No 74 was among the first batch of properties inspected after landslides, triggered by record rainfall in September 2023, exposed unauthorised building works at homes along the estate’s seafront.
The court heard that the illegal additions encroached on government land and that part of the retaining wall along the slope had also been unlawfully demolished to make way for a basement.
Evidence showed that the expansion was completed around 2020, with photographs showing the illegal structures were already in place at that time.
Cheng said that even though the land authorities served the defendant a notice two years ago ordering the removal of the additional structures, the illegal works remained in place.
Yuen reiterated that the company had filed an appeal with the Appeal Tribunal (Buildings), where she hoped to resolve how the structures could be demolished safely.
She told the magistrate that construction professionals estimated it would cost HK$50 million to restore the house to its original floor plan, citing a high risk to safety.
Yuen said the company would prefer to wait for the tribunal’s decision before developing a comprehensive plan to fix the property.
Cheng noted that all other Redhill Peninsula homeowners who breached the building rules had either removed all the illegal structures or had projects under way.
He noted that the defendant had not even taken the first step to remove parts that were relatively easy to tear down.
“This is extremely serious, [the defendant] has shown a total disregard for law and safety,” he said.
For unlawfully occupying government land, Cheng imposed a HK$500,000 fine, which is half the maximum penalty for conviction.
After considering the degree of violation for each illegal structure – including additions to the entrance and bedroom floors, a glass box erected in the garden, and alterations to the retaining wall and basement floors below the garden – Cheng issued fines ranging from HK$30,000 to HK$200,000 for the six summary offences.
Cheng fined the defendant a total of HK$980,000 across seven charges and ordered payment within 14 days.
During inspections at Redhill Peninsula, authorities found that 70 of the 85 seaside homes had extended beyond their legal limits, built unauthorised structures, or both.
In January, the Buildings Department said it had launched prosecutions against the owners of 30 detached houses at Redhill Peninsula, with 24 convictions so far.
The South China Morning Post confirmed with prosecutors that Future Ocean was the only offender that had denied responsibility.
Just two weeks ago, Sunset Boulevard Company, which owns House No 76, was fined HK$110,000 for building illegal structures and a swimming pool in the garden. The court earlier heard that the homeowner had already applied to the Buildings Department to remove the illegal structures, with works under way.

data.title: BYD, China’s EV king, posts a 55% slump in first-quarter profit
data.description:
BYD’s first-quarter net profit matched market estimates as China’s electric vehicle (EV) king partly offset weakness in its domestic market with rising exports.
The mainland’s largest and most profitable EV maker reported a net profit of nearly 4.09 billion yuan (US$590 million) for the first three months of the year, a drop of 55 per cent compared with a year earlier.
Revenue came in at 150.2 billion yuan, down 11.8 per cent from a year earlier.
The market’s consensus estimate was 4.09 billion yuan for net profit and 140 billion yuan for revenue.
The first-quarter revenue was the lowest quarterly figure since the April to June period in 2024, while the net profit was the least since the first quarter of 2023, as slowing growth at home weighed on earnings.
Rising overseas sales helped the Shenzhen-based carmaker offset weakness at home. BYD exported 120,083 units in March, marking the fifth consecutive month with shipments of more than 100,000 vehicles.
Notably, BYD’s exports in the first quarter accounted for nearly 46 per cent of its total deliveries, double the full-year proportion of 23 per cent in 2025.

Although BYD did not reveal its overseas profit margin, it is widely believed that Chinese EV makers could earn more abroad than at home, where they have been embroiled in a vicious price war. Since last year top policymakers and regulators have repeatedly urged firms to end the profit-squeezing competition and seek further technological breakthroughs.
On the mainland, carmakers’ average net margin per vehicle stood at about 5,000 yuan, according to Nick Lai, head of auto research for Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan Chase.
In October, he said that the margin could rise fourfold to 20,000 yuan in overseas markets, where Chinese cars commanded higher prices.
The energy shock arising from the Middle East conflict is also expected to help EV makers boost overseas sales.
Morgan Stanley cut its forecast for China’s domestic EV sales growth to flat this year compared with a 7 per cent increase previously, according to a report published on April 22. The US bank expects car exports to grow 88 per cent compared with its earlier forecast of 38 per cent.
BYD told analysts in late March that its outbound shipments could rise to 1.5 million units this year compared with its previous target of 1.3 million announced in January.
Moreover, the EV king in the mass market is stepping up efforts to tap the premium category in a bid for higher profit margins. It plans to debut its Denza Z all-electric sports car in Europe in July and is examining options to enter Formula 1 racing.
In early April, BYD said it would launch the Denza Z9GT at prices starting from €103,500 (US$121,050), nearly triple the price at home.
China halved the sales tax exemption for EVs in January, leading to a 23.8 per cent dip in domestic sales in the first quarter.
BYD also saw its total EV sales decrease 30 per cent year on year to around 1 million units in the first quarter. Even with an increase in March, the monthly figure was still the second lowest in 15 months.

data.title: DeepSeek mystery: who is speaking for start-up amid CEO Liang Wenfeng’s absence?
data.description:
The potential emergence of a new DeepSeek spokesman has sparked intrigue as speculation over the whereabouts of founder and CEO Liang Wenfeng continues, more than a year after his last public appearance.
While the Hangzhou start-up’s latest V4 model did not make the same waves as its breakout moment a year earlier, the much-anticipated release still grabbed headlines through a collaboration with domestic tech giant Huawei Technologies and its remarkably low prices.
For the traditionally low-profile firm, a recent change to its shareholding structure suggests that Liang’s control over the company has not waned. Still, developments in recent days point to a new public face for the start-up.
Where is Liang Wenfeng?
Liang has not appeared publicly since a high-profile televised meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping in February last year, immediately after the release of DeepSeek’s R1 model. Yet there were no signs that his leading role had changed.
According to corporate database Qichacha, Liang’s equity stake had recently increased to 34 per cent from 1 per cent, with his subscribed capital jumping from 100,000 yuan (US$14,630) to 5.1 million yuan. DeepSeek’s registered capital also rose from 10 million yuan to 15 million yuan.

The change in shareholding came as DeepSeek began raising external capital for the first time, the South China Morning Post reported last week, citing people familiar with the matter. Under China’s company law, approval by shareholders representing more than two-thirds of voting rights is needed for key company decisions.
Who is Chen Deli?
Senior researcher Chen Deli was among a few DeepSeek personnel to post publicly on their own personal X accounts on Friday following the V4’s release. He wrote to his almost 24,000 followers: “484 days later, we humbly share our labour of love. As always, we stay true to long-termism and open source for all.”
The short post contrasted with DeepSeek’s online presence in previous years, when several researchers were active on X, the most popular online gathering place for artificial intelligence researchers.
According to his archived profile on coding platform GitHub, Chen completed both his undergraduate and master’s studies at Peking University, where he was a member of the famed Language Computing and Machine Learning Group (Lanco Lab) at the School of Computer Science.
The lab boasts several alumni now leading AI development efforts at major Chinese companies, including Xiaomi’s Luo Fuli and Alibaba Group Holding’s former Qwen team technical lead Lin Junyang.
Since joining DeepSeek in 2023, Chen’s name has appeared in every major model release from the firm, including V3, R1 and V4. According to Google Scholar, Chen had more than 22,000 citations to his name, placing him on the same level of academic impact as tenured professors.
What has Chen said?
Chen’s first major public appearance was in March 2024, when he spoke at US chip giant Nvidia’s flagship GTC conference on behalf of DeepSeek and its parent company High-Flyer Quant.
In a 20-minute video presentation, Chen explained the challenge of aligning AI models with diverse human values, proposing instead a “decoupling” framework that allowed for “cultural value diversity”.
His next major appearance was in November, when he stood in for Liang at a state-backed industry event alongside the heads of five other companies, which together with DeepSeek are collectively known as China’s “Six Little Dragons”.
In remarks that went viral, Chen told industry peers and government officials he was pessimistic about AI’s overall impact on society, warning that most jobs could eventually be automated and calling for AI companies to act as “whistle-blowers” by telling the public which jobs would be made redundant first.
Is DeepSeek losing its star researchers?
Given China’s cutthroat competition for AI talent, there has been constant speculation about whether DeepSeek could keep its star researchers out of the hands of Big Tech firms with much deeper pockets.
The V4 release provided some answers. According to the technical paper accompanying the model, DeepSeek’s research and engineering team had expanded to 270 staff, up from 212 in early December, a gain of more than 27 per cent.
Crucially, the paper confirmed that most of DeepSeek’s 18 “core contributors” to its groundbreaking R1 model were still at the company. Only two had departed: Guo Daya, who joined TikTok owner ByteDance, according to domestic media reports, and Zhang Haowei, whose next destination remained unknown.
